author: @LoremIpsum1970
text: Hope you stick with this series because functional resin print videos aren't that common. One thing to watch for is going to be the ideal print temp for the resin VOG did an enclosure heater not long ago, Thomas Sanladerer did trials of fillers one being graphite for conductive resins, and finally, Siraya-Tech resins would be a good start to play with as you can mix them to fine-tune their properties and they're not too expensive but sensitive to temperatures...

author: @donaldhanson2908
text:
I was wondering about all that gating.
It seemed a lot for such a small part.
it will need a lot of chasing to get out and smoothed.

author: @lylehintz7275
text:
Did you need to refill the resin as the print progressed?

Is there a limit to the vertical size or volume of the print?

replies.author: @YearRoundHibernater
replies.text:
no your slicing software tells you how much resin the print itself takes, and you just make sure you have more then enough to keep a full layer on. If in doubt go close to the max level, you can always pour the unused resin through a paint filter cone (or I use a metal drip coffee filter) and back into the bottle.
There is a limit on the verticle height yes (although it prints from the bottom up so I think I've seen people modify them to print much higher but it's a lot of modification work) although you can split a model easy enough in software and print it in separate pieces and glue them together after, would effect the structure.

text: Nice guide rob. I think we're going to see a new level of creativity from you soon...hehe no pressure I'm looking forward to seeing some graphene experiments. I'd bet it would increase the strength of the plastic. Ive always wondered if eletcric motor technology would turn towards 3d printed graphene coils to replace the copper coils.
